summary: Gruber uses a fictional dialogue between Avie Tevanian and a QA tester to satirize Apple's dismissive attitude toward Mac OS 9 compatibility during the Jaguar release. The comedy centers on the Jaguar install disc shipping with a readme PDF that can't be double-clicked to open under OS 9, because Apple didn't bother setting HFS type/creator metadata. Each time the QA tester raises a legitimate concern, Avie dismisses it as cosmetic or deprecated. The irony is that the 'Welcome to Mac OS X' folder's readme file only works if you're already running Mac OS X. Gruber uses the format to highlight Apple's increasingly hostile posture toward its own legacy OS during the transition period.
key_points: The Jaguar install disc shipped with a readme PDF that couldn't be opened by double-clicking under Mac OS 9 due to missing HFS type/creator metadata
Apple dismissed type/creator codes as deprecated even though the install disc needed to boot from OS 9
The circular absurdity: the fix instructions would need to go in the readme, which is the file users can't open
The piece satirizes Apple's philosophy of using broken backward compatibility as implicit encouragement to upgrade to OS X
money_quotes: Set the type and creator. So it'll open in Acrobat.
It's the readme file, the first file most users will try to open, in a folder named 'Welcome to Mac OS X', but it can only be double-clicked if you're already running Mac OS X.
There's the lesson: 'Everything works better on OS X.'
We'll put it in the readme.
This isn't getting fixed, is it?
key_insight: Apple's eagerness to deprecate Mac OS 9 conventions led to the absurd situation where the Jaguar install disc's own readme file was broken for the very users it was supposed to welcome to OS X.
